enrichment.short_description: Traggo is a free, open-source, self-hosted time tracking tool that logs time spans using flexible tags instead of fixed tasks or projects.
enrichment.seo_title: Traggo Review: Self-Hosted Tag-Based Time Tracking
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Traggo is a free, open-source, self-hosted time tracker that uses flexible tags instead of tasks. See features, setup requirements, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Traggo is a free, open-source, self-hosted time tracking application built with a Go backend and released under the GPL-3.0 license. Unlike conventional time trackers such as Toggl Track or Clockify that organize time around fixed projects and tasks, Traggo uses a tag-based model: every logged time span can carry multiple key-value tags (for example project, client, or activity type), which users can then filter and analyze in custom reports and statistics views. Because there is no predefined task hierarchy, Traggo adapts to almost any workflow without requiring users to pre-plan a project structure. The application ships as a Docker container, making it straightforward to self-host on a home server, NAS, or VPS, and it exposes a GraphQL API for integrations and custom tooling. Traggo supports multiple users on a single instance, a visual timeline for reviewing logged spans, and tag-based statistics for understanding how time is distributed. As with most self-hosted, open-source tools, there is no vendor cloud, subscription fee, or third-party data access involved; users control their own infrastructure and data entirely. The project originated as a community open-source effort (hosted under the traggo GitHub organization) rather than a funded company, and active development has continued through community forks such as Lutonite/traggo. Traggo is best compared to other self-hosted time trackers like Kimai, and to cloud services like Toggl Track or Clockify for teams who prioritize data ownership and tag-based flexibility over managed convenience. It has no official pricing since it is free to run; the only cost is the infrastructure needed to host it.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Traggo
paragraphs: Traggo is an open-source, self-hosted time tracking application designed around tags rather than tasks or projects. Instead of creating a project hierarchy before you can log time, you simply start and stop a time span and attach any number of key-value tags to it, such as project equals website-redesign or type equals meeting. This tag-first approach makes Traggo especially flexible for freelancers, consultants, and small teams whose work does not fit neatly into a single fixed taxonomy.

Because Traggo is self-hosted, it appeals strongly to privacy-conscious users and homelab enthusiasts who do not want their time-tracking data stored on a third-party server. The application is distributed as a Docker image, so deployment typically involves running a container on a personal server, NAS device, or cloud VPS rather than signing up for a hosted account. There is no official managed cloud version; running an instance is the only way to use Traggo.

The project is maintained as open-source software under the GPL-3.0 license, with source code split across a Go-based server and a web-based client. Development has continued through the community, including maintained forks that keep dependencies and features current after periods of slower activity on the original repository.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Traggo's core feature is its tag-based data model: every tracked time span can carry multiple tags with arbitrary keys and values, letting users slice their time data along whatever dimensions matter to them, whether that is client, project, task type, or billing status. A built-in reporting view aggregates logged time by tag, making it possible to see, for example, total hours per client or per activity category without any manual spreadsheet work.

The application includes a visual timeline for reviewing and editing past entries, keyboard-driven quick entry for fast logging, and multi-user support so a small team can share a single self-hosted instance while keeping their own tagged history. A GraphQL API is available for developers who want to build custom integrations, scripts, or dashboards on top of their tracked data.

Because everything runs on infrastructure the user controls, Traggo avoids the recurring subscription costs, download limits, and data-export restrictions that are common with commercial, cloud-hosted time trackers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Traggo itself is completely free and always has been, since it is released as open-source software with no paid tiers, license keys, or feature gates. The only cost associated with using Traggo is whatever it costs to run the Docker container, whether that is a spare home server, a NAS, or a small cloud VPS, typically a few dollars a month at most for personal use.

features.title: Tag-based time entries
features.description: Log time spans and attach unlimited custom key-value tags instead of choosing from a fixed project or task list.

features.title: Timeline visualization
features.description: Review and edit logged time spans on a visual timeline for quick auditing and corrections.

features.title: Tag-based reporting
features.description: Generate statistics and reports aggregated by any tag combination, such as client or activity type.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Run a single self-hosted instance shared by multiple users, each with their own tracked history.

features.title: GraphQL API
features.description: Query and manipulate time-tracking data programmatically for custom integrations and dashboards.

features.title: Docker-based deployment
features.description: Ships as a Docker container for straightforward self-hosting on a server, NAS, or VPS.

features.title: Open-source, GPL-3.0 licensed
features.description: Full source code is publicly available and free to modify, audit, or self-host indefinitely.

features.title: Data ownership and privacy
features.description: All tracked time data stays on infrastructure the user controls, with no third-party access.

pros: Completely free with no subscription fees or feature paywalls
Full control and privacy over tracked time data through self-hosting
Highly flexible tag-based model adapts to almost any workflow
Lightweight Docker deployment makes self-hosting relatively simple
Open-source codebase can be audited, modified, or extended by anyone
cons: Requires technical comfort with Docker and self-hosting to get started
No official managed cloud option for users who prefer not to self-host
No official native mobile apps, relying on the browser-based web client
Smaller community and slower release cadence than commercial time trackers
Lacks built-in invoicing or client-billing tools found in commercial alternatives

faqs.question: Is Traggo free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Traggo is fully open-source under the GPL-3.0 license with no paid plans; the only cost is hosting it yourself.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Traggo?
faqs.answer: Yes, Traggo has no official hosted cloud service, so you need to run it yourself, typically via Docker on a server, NAS, or VPS.

faqs.question: How does Traggo differ from tools like Toggl Track?
faqs.answer: Traggo replaces the traditional project and task hierarchy with flexible tags, and it is self-hosted rather than a paid cloud subscription.

faqs.question: Does Traggo support multiple users?
faqs.answer: Yes, a single self-hosted Traggo instance can support multiple user accounts, each tracking their own time.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app for Traggo?
faqs.answer: There is no official native mobile app; Traggo is used through its web-based interface, which is accessible from a mobile browser.

faqs.question: Does Traggo have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, Traggo exposes a GraphQL API that developers can use to build custom integrations or automate data entry.

faqs.question: Who maintains Traggo?
faqs.answer: Traggo is a community-driven open-source project hosted under the traggo GitHub organization, with active forks continuing development.

faqs.question: What technology is Traggo built on?
faqs.answer: Traggo uses a Go-based backend server and a web client, and it is distributed as a Docker container for easy deployment.

use_cases.title: Freelance and consulting time tracking
use_cases.description: Freelancers tag time entries by client and project to track billable hours without committing to a rigid project structure.

use_cases.title: Privacy-first self-hosted tooling
use_cases.description: Small teams or self-hosters run Traggo alongside other self-hosted apps to keep time-tracking data entirely off third-party servers.

use_cases.title: Personal productivity tracking
use_cases.description: Individuals track how their time is spent across work, learning, and personal activities using flexible custom tags.

enrichment.short_description: Transistor is a bootstrapped podcast hosting and analytics platform that lets creators publish unlimited shows and distribute to every major podcast app from one dashboard.
enrichment.seo_title: Transistor.fm Review: Podcast Hosting and Analytics
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Transistor is a podcast hosting platform with unlimited shows, downloads-based pricing from 19 dollars a month, analytics, and private podcast support.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Transistor.fm is a podcast hosting and analytics platform built by Justin Jackson and Jon Buda, who met at the XOXO Festival in Portland in 2014 and officially launched the product on August 1, 2018. The company is bootstrapped rather than venture-funded and operates as a small, distributed team with a nominal headquarters in Chicago, Illinois. Transistor's key differentiator is its pricing model: rather than charging per podcast show, as many competitors like Libsyn or Buzzsprout historically have, Transistor allows unlimited podcasts and unlimited episode storage on every plan, with price instead scaling by monthly download volume. Plans are Starter at 19 dollars a month for up to 10,000 downloads, Professional at 49 dollars a month for up to 50,000 downloads, and Business at 99 dollars a month for up to 150,000 downloads, with additional download blocks billed beyond those limits. Every plan includes one-click distribution to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other major directories via RSS, a built-in podcast website with SEO-friendly episode pages, an embeddable audio player, private podcast support for premium or membership content, team member accounts, and an analytics dashboard built around IAB-certified download measurement. This unlimited-shows structure makes Transistor a popular choice for podcast networks and agencies managing several shows under one account, as well as solo creators who want simple, predictable pricing. It is commonly compared to Buzzsprout, Libsyn, Podbean, and Captivate, with Transistor generally seen as a more modern, design-forward alternative aimed at professional and business podcasters rather than absolute beginners on a shoestring budget, since it has no permanent free tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Transistor
paragraphs: Transistor is a podcast hosting platform that stores, distributes, and reports on podcast audio and video for independent creators, businesses, and podcast networks. Founded in 2018 by Justin Jackson and Jon Buda as a bootstrapped company, Transistor built its reputation on a simple promise: host as many podcasts as you want on a single account, with pricing based on how many downloads your shows generate rather than how many shows you run.

That unlimited-podcasts model sets Transistor apart from many legacy hosts that charge separately for each additional show, and it has made the platform particularly popular with podcast networks, agencies, and marketing teams that manage multiple shows for different clients or brands from one dashboard.

Beyond hosting, Transistor provides each podcast with an automatically generated, SEO-optimized website, distribution to every major podcast directory through standard RSS feeds, and analytics designed to meet Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) measurement standards, which matters for shows selling sponsorships.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every Transistor plan includes unlimited podcasts and unlimited episode storage, with the only variable being monthly download volume. Shows are automatically submitted and distributed to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other directories through RSS, and each podcast gets a customizable, SEO-friendly website and episode pages without any extra setup.

For creators monetizing beyond advertising, Transistor supports private podcast feeds that can be gated behind a login or subscription, making it a common choice for premium content, internal company podcasts, or paid membership shows. Team accounts let multiple collaborators manage episodes, and the platform includes an embeddable web player for placing episodes directly on external websites.

The analytics dashboard reports downloads, listener geography, and platform breakdowns using IAB-certified methodology, giving shows credible numbers to share with sponsors. An API is also available for developers who want to automate publishing or pull analytics into other systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Transistor prices its three core plans by monthly downloads rather than number of shows: Starter at 19 dollars a month includes up to 10,000 downloads, Professional at 49 dollars a month includes up to 50,000 downloads, and Business at 99 dollars a month includes up to 150,000 downloads. All three tiers include unlimited podcasts, unlimited episodes, and the full feature set, with higher tiers adding capabilities like additional team seats and advanced customization; usage beyond a plan's download allowance is billed as an overage.
There is no permanent free plan, but Transistor commonly offers a free trial period so prospective customers can test the platform before committing to a monthly subscription.

features.title: Unlimited podcasts and episodes
features.description: Host as many shows and episodes as needed on a single account at every pricing tier.

features.title: Downloads-based pricing
features.description: Plans scale by monthly download volume rather than by number of podcasts hosted.

features.title: Built-in podcast websites
features.description: Every show gets an automatically generated, SEO-friendly website and episode pages.

features.title: Private podcasts
features.description: Gate premium or membership content behind login-protected private RSS feeds.

features.title: Team accounts
features.description: Add collaborators with shared access to manage episodes across an organization.

features.title: IAB-certified analytics
features.description: Track downloads, listener geography, and platform breakdowns using industry-standard measurement.

features.title: Embeddable player and API
features.description: Embed episodes anywhere on the web and automate publishing through Transistor's API.

pros: Unlimited shows and episodes on every plan, ideal for networks and agencies
Simple, predictable downloads-based pricing instead of per-show fees
Clean, modern interface and automatically generated podcast websites
Independently owned and bootstrapped, without ad-driven business incentives
Strong support for private, premium, and membership podcasts
cons: No permanent free plan, unlike some entry-level competitors
Entry pricing is higher than budget hosts aimed at hobbyist podcasters
Download overage fees can increase costs for fast-growing shows
Fewer built-in monetization or sponsorship marketplace tools than some rivals
No self-hosting option since it is a fully managed cloud service

faqs.question: Is there a free plan for Transistor?
faqs.answer: Transistor does not offer a permanent free plan, but it typically provides a free trial for new users to test the platform.

faqs.question: How is Transistor's pricing calculated?
faqs.answer: Pricing is based on monthly podcast downloads rather than the number of shows, so every plan allows unlimited podcasts and episodes.

faqs.question: Can I host multiple podcasts on one Transistor account?
faqs.answer: Yes, unlimited podcasts and episodes are included on every plan, which is a key differentiator from many competitors.

faqs.question: Does Transistor support private or premium podcasts?
faqs.answer: Yes, Transistor supports private RSS feeds for gated, subscription, or membership podcast content.

faqs.question: Where does Transistor distribute podcasts?
faqs.answer: Episodes are distributed via RSS to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other major podcast directories.

faqs.question: Who founded Transistor?
faqs.answer: Transistor was founded by Justin Jackson and Jon Buda, who launched the product on August 1, 2018.

faqs.question: Is Transistor venture-backed?
faqs.answer: No, Transistor is a bootstrapped, independently owned company rather than a venture-funded startup.

faqs.question: Does Transistor provide podcast analytics?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes an IAB-certified analytics dashboard covering downloads, listener location, and app breakdowns.

enrichment.short_description: Transmission is a free, open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client known for its lightweight footprint, clean interface, and native apps for macOS, Linux, and other Unix systems.
enrichment.seo_title: Transmission BitTorrent Client: Free and Open Source
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Transmission is a free, open-source BitTorrent client for macOS, Linux, and Unix, known for its lightweight design and clean interface since 2005.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Transmission is a free, open-source BitTorrent client whose development began as a volunteer-driven project in 2005. It is released under the GNU General Public License, with some components under the MIT License, and is maintained today by a community of contributors rather than a company. Transmission offers native applications for macOS and various Linux and Unix desktop environments, a lightweight command-line daemon for headless servers and NAS devices, and a built-in web interface for remote management, making it one of the most widely deployed BitTorrent clients on self-hosted infrastructure. It gained early prominence when Ubuntu adopted it as the default BitTorrent client in January 2008, and it remains the default or a commonly bundled client on distributions including Linux Mint, Fedora, and the GNOME flavor of openSUSE. The project is known for its minimalist philosophy: no ads, no bundled toolbars, and no monetization schemes, in contrast to some historically ad-supported clients like uTorrent. Version 4.0, released in 2023, was a major milestone that added support for BitTorrent v2 and hybrid torrents, IPv6 peer blocklists, and a completely redesigned, mobile-friendly web client, and the project has continued shipping incremental 4.x releases since. Transmission is typically compared to qBittorrent, which offers a broader built-in feature set such as integrated search and RSS automation, and to uTorrent, which has drawn criticism over the years for bundled software and ads; Transmission's appeal instead rests on its simplicity, low resource usage, and trustworthiness as a genuinely free and open tool, particularly for server and NAS-based torrenting.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Transmission
paragraphs: Transmission is a free, open-source BitTorrent client that has been in active, volunteer-led development since 2005. It provides a cross-platform back-end paired with multiple front ends, including native macOS and Linux/Unix desktop applications, a lightweight headless daemon for servers, and a web-based interface for remote control from any browser.

The client became widely known after Ubuntu made it the default BitTorrent application in January 2008, and it has since remained a standard or commonly recommended client across many Linux distributions, prized for being simple to install and use without configuration overhead.

Unlike some commercial torrent clients, Transmission carries no advertising, no bundled third-party software, and no premium upsells. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License with select components under the MIT License, and its source code is maintained publicly on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Transmission is built to be resource-light, running efficiently on everything from an aging laptop to a low-power NAS device, which is part of why it is a popular pick for always-on, server-based torrenting. Its daemon mode allows it to run headless, controlled entirely through its built-in web client or third-party remote apps, without requiring a desktop environment.

With the release of version 4.0, Transmission added support for the BitTorrent v2 protocol and hybrid torrents, IPv6-aware peer blocklists, and a ground-up redesign of its web interface with full mobile support, modernizing a client that had historically prioritized stability over new features.

Core functionality includes bandwidth scheduling, peer encryption options, watch folders for automatically adding new torrent files, and a straightforward queueing system, all wrapped in an interface deliberately kept simpler than more feature-dense competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Transmission is entirely free to download and use, with no paid tiers, license fees, or in-app purchases of any kind. As a volunteer-maintained open-source project, its ongoing development is sustained by contributor time rather than a commercial revenue model, which is also why the application contains no ads or bundled software.

features.title: Native macOS and Linux/Unix apps
features.description: Dedicated desktop clients built for macOS and multiple Linux and Unix environments.

features.title: Lightweight resource usage
features.description: Runs efficiently on low-power hardware, including NAS devices and older machines.

features.title: Built-in web interface
features.description: Manage torrents remotely from any browser through Transmission's own web client.

features.title: Headless daemon mode
features.description: Runs as a background service on servers without requiring a desktop environment.

features.title: BitTorrent v2 and hybrid torrent support
features.description: Added in version 4.0, supporting the newer BitTorrent v2 protocol alongside legacy v1 torrents.

features.title: IPv6 peer support and blocklists
features.description: Handles IPv6 peers and supports IP-based blocklists for filtering unwanted connections.

features.title: No ads or bundled software
features.description: Ships completely clean, with no advertising, toolbars, or bundled third-party installers.

features.title: Open-source GPL license
features.description: Publicly available source code that anyone can inspect, audit, or contribute to.

pros: Completely free with no ads, upsells, or bundled software
Very lightweight and efficient, well suited to NAS and server use
Trusted default client on many major Linux distributions
Headless daemon and web interface make remote management easy
Actively maintained open-source project with regular releases
cons: Fewer advanced features than qBittorrent, such as integrated search or deeper RSS automation
Interface is intentionally minimal and can feel dated to some users
No official first-party Windows build, requiring community alternatives
Release cadence can be slower than some actively funded competitors
Limited built-in customization compared to more configurable clients

faqs.question: Is Transmission free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Transmission is entirely free, open-source software with no paid tiers or hidden costs.

faqs.question: What platforms does Transmission support?
faqs.answer: Transmission offers native apps for macOS and various Linux and Unix systems, plus a headless daemon and web interface usable from any browser.

faqs.question: Does Transmission contain ads or bundled software?
faqs.answer: No, Transmission ships completely clean with no advertising or bundled third-party installers.

faqs.question: Can I run Transmission on a NAS or home server?
faqs.answer: Yes, its lightweight daemon mode is commonly used on NAS devices and servers, controlled through the built-in web interface.

faqs.question: Does Transmission support BitTorrent v2?
faqs.answer: Yes, support for BitTorrent v2 and hybrid torrents was added in the version 4.0 release.

faqs.question: Who develops Transmission?
faqs.answer: Transmission is developed and maintained by a community of volunteer open-source contributors.

faqs.question: Is there an official Windows version of Transmission?
faqs.answer: Transmission has not historically offered an official first-party Windows client, and Windows users often rely on community-built alternatives.

faqs.question: How does Transmission compare to qBittorrent or uTorrent?
faqs.answer: Transmission is lighter and cleaner than uTorrent, which has drawn criticism for bundled software, and simpler than qBittorrent, which offers a denser built-in feature set.

use_cases.title: NAS and home server torrenting
use_cases.description: Run Transmission headless on a NAS or home server, managed remotely through its web interface.

use_cases.title: Lightweight desktop torrenting
use_cases.description: Use the native macOS or Linux app for everyday torrent downloads without a heavy resource footprint.

use_cases.title: Default distribution torrent client
use_cases.description: Ship or install Transmission as the trusted, ad-free default BitTorrent client on a Linux distribution.

features: Full BitTorrent client with no ads
BitTorrent v2 and hybrid torrent support

enrichment.short_description: Tray.io, rebranded Tray.ai, is an enterprise integration platform as a service (iPaaS) that lets technical and business teams build API integrations, workflow automations, and AI agents with a visual, low-code builder.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tray.io, now Tray.ai, is an enterprise integration and automation platform with visual workflow building and AI agent capabilities. See pricing and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tray.io was founded in 2012 by Rich Waldron, Alistair Russell, and Dominic Lewis, who met while studying together in the UK and initially ran a web agency before building the platform. The company is now headquartered in San Francisco, California, and has raised more than 100 million dollars in venture funding from investors including Spark Capital, True Ventures, and GGV Capital, with a valuation reported around 600 million dollars. Between 2018 and 2022 the company grew from roughly 500,000 dollars to approximately 50 million dollars in annual recurring revenue. In 2024, Tray.io rebranded to Tray.ai to reflect a strategic pivot toward AI-native automation, while keeping its core positioning as an enterprise integration platform as a service, or iPaaS, and has been recognized as a two-time Visionary in Gartner's Magic Quadrant for iPaaS. The platform provides a visual, low-code workflow builder, a universal connector framework covering hundreds of pre-built integrations, and, post-rebrand, tools for building AI agents that can act across connected systems. Tray targets mid-market and enterprise customers rather than the self-serve small-business audience that tools like Zapier or Make focus on, offering deeper governance features such as single sign-on, audit logging, and multiple workspaces for departmental separation. Pricing is entirely sales-assisted with no public self-serve rate or free tier; published tiers include a Pro plan built around 250,000 starter task credits and three workspaces, a Team plan with 500,000 credits and twenty workspaces, and an Enterprise plan with 750,000-plus credits, unlimited workspaces, and dedicated customer success support, with third-party sources estimating Pro-level contracts starting in the range of several hundred dollars per month and Enterprise contracts reaching tens of thousands of dollars annually.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tray.io
paragraphs: Tray.io, now marketed as Tray.ai following a 2024 rebrand, is an integration platform as a service designed for mid-market and enterprise organizations that need to connect SaaS applications, automate cross-system workflows, and increasingly, build AI-driven agents that act across those connected tools. It was founded in 2012 in the UK by Rich Waldron, Alistair Russell, and Dominic Lewis and is now headquartered in San Francisco.

Unlike simpler automation tools built for self-serve, small-business use, Tray positions itself around a universal connector framework and a visual, low-code builder that gives technical teams the flexibility to handle complex logic, custom API calls, and enterprise governance requirements that lighter tools were not designed for.

The company has raised significant venture capital and has been recognized by Gartner as a Visionary in its Magic Quadrant for iPaaS, reflecting its focus on serving RevOps, IT, and platform engineering teams inside larger organizations rather than individual freelancers or very small businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Tray is a visual workflow builder that lets users design multi-step automations with branching logic, loops, and custom code steps, backed by a universal connector framework that supports hundreds of pre-built application connections alongside the ability to call any REST or GraphQL API directly. Following its rebrand to Tray.ai, the platform also added capabilities for building and deploying AI agents that can reason over data and take actions across connected systems.

Enterprise governance is a major differentiator: Tray supports single sign-on and SAML authentication, detailed audit logging, and multiple isolated workspaces so different departments or business units can manage their own automations without interfering with one another. A task-credit-based usage model underlies billing across all plans.

Higher tiers add dedicated customer success management, service-level agreement guarantees, and expanded connector and workspace limits, positioning the platform for organizations running dozens or hundreds of interconnected automations across critical business systems like Salesforce, NetSuite, and Marketo.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tray does not publish self-serve pricing and requires a sales conversation to receive a quote, in contrast to self-serve competitors like Zapier or Make. Published plan tiers are Pro, with 250,000 starter task credits and three workspaces, Team, with 500,000 starter task credits and twenty workspaces, and Enterprise, with 750,000-plus starter task credits, unlimited workspaces, and additional security and support features.

Third-party pricing trackers report that Pro-level contracts commonly start in the range of several hundred dollars per month, Team plans often start around 1,500 dollars per month, and Enterprise agreements can begin around 36,000 dollars annually for smaller enterprise deployments, though actual pricing depends on negotiated task volume and contract terms.

features.title: Visual low-code workflow builder
features.description: Design multi-step automations with branching logic, loops, and custom code steps in a visual canvas.

features.title: Universal connector framework
features.description: Connect to hundreds of pre-built SaaS integrations plus any custom REST or GraphQL API.

features.title: AI agent building tools
features.description: Build and deploy AI agents that can reason over data and take actions across connected systems, added under the Tray.ai rebrand.

features.title: Enterprise governance
features.description: Single sign-on, SAML authentication, and detailed audit logging for enterprise security requirements.

features.title: Multiple workspaces
features.description: Isolate automations by department or business unit within a single account.

features.title: Task-credit usage model
features.description: Billing is based on task credits consumed by workflow executions rather than flat per-user pricing.

features.title: Dedicated customer success
features.description: Higher tiers include a dedicated customer success manager and service-level agreement guarantees.

features.title: API-first architecture
features.description: Built for developers who need deep, custom integration logic beyond simple point-and-click connectors.

pros: Highly flexible for complex, custom enterprise integration logic
Strong governance and security features including SSO and audit logs
Universal connector approach supports virtually any API, not just pre-built apps
Backed by substantial venture funding and Gartner Magic Quadrant recognition
Increasingly AI-native automation capabilities following the Tray.ai rebrand
cons: No free plan or transparent self-serve pricing published on the website
Requires a sales conversation to get any quote, slowing evaluation
Steeper learning curve than self-serve tools like Zapier or Make
Cost is generally prohibitive for small businesses and solo users
Task-credit pricing can be difficult to forecast accurately at scale

faqs.question: What is Tray.io or Tray.ai?
faqs.answer: It is an enterprise integration platform as a service, or iPaaS, that lets teams build API integrations, workflow automations, and AI agents with a visual, low-code builder.

faqs.question: Who founded Tray.io?
faqs.answer: Tray.io was founded in 2012 by Rich Waldron, Alistair Russell, and Dominic Lewis.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan for Tray.io?
faqs.answer: No, Tray.io does not offer a free plan or public self-serve signup.

faqs.question: How much does Tray.io cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is custom and sales-assisted, based on task-credit tiers; third-party estimates put Pro plans starting around several hundred dollars per month, scaling to tens of thousands annually for Enterprise.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Tray.io and Tray.ai?
faqs.answer: They are the same company; Tray.io rebranded to Tray.ai in 2024 to reflect its focus on AI agent-building capabilities.

faqs.question: How does Tray.io compare to Zapier or Make?
faqs.answer: Tray targets mid-market and enterprise customers with deeper governance and custom logic, while Zapier and Make focus on self-serve small-business automation.

faqs.question: Does Tray.io support AI agents?
faqs.answer: Yes, following its rebrand to Tray.ai, the platform includes tools for building AI agents that act across connected systems.

faqs.question: Has Tray.io been recognized by Gartner?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has been named a Visionary in Gartner's Magic Quadrant for iPaaS on multiple occasions.

use_cases.title: Enterprise RevOps automation
use_cases.description: Automate workflows across Salesforce, Marketo, NetSuite, and other revenue systems with custom logic and governance.

use_cases.title: AI agent workflows for engineering teams
use_cases.description: Build AI agents that act across connected SaaS tools using Tray's low-code, API-first builder.

enrichment.short_description: Trello is a visual, Kanban-style project management and collaboration tool that organizes work into boards, lists, and cards, owned by Atlassian since 2017.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Trello Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Trello is Atlassian's Kanban-style project management tool. Compare its Free, Standard, Premium, and Enterprise plans, features, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Trello was founded in 2011 by Joel Spolsky and Michael Pryor as a product of Fog Creek Software in New York City. It was spun out into an independent company, Trello Inc., in 2014 and was acquired by Atlassian in January 2017 for approximately 425 million dollars, where it remains a core product alongside Jira and Confluence. Trello popularized the Kanban board metaphor for everyday task and project management, organizing work into boards, lists, and cards that users can drag between stages. Its built-in automation engine, called Butler, lets users create no-code rules, buttons, and scheduled actions without any paid add-on, and its Power-Ups system provides integrations with tools like Slack, Google Drive, and Jira. As of 2026, Trello offers four pricing tiers: Free, which includes up to 10 boards per workspace, 10 collaborators, and 250 automation runs per month; Standard at 5 dollars per user per month billed annually, adding unlimited boards and advanced checklists; Premium at 10 dollars per user per month billed annually, adding Calendar, Timeline, Table, and Dashboard views along with admin controls; and Enterprise at 17.50 dollars per user per month billed annually with a 50-user minimum, adding organization-wide security and permission controls. Trello is frequently compared to Asana, Monday.com, ClickUp, and its own sibling product Jira; it is generally regarded as the simplest and most approachable of these tools, ideal for small teams and personal task management, while more complex, resource-heavy projects often outgrow its relatively lightweight reporting and dependency-tracking capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Trello
paragraphs: Trello is a visual project management and team collaboration tool built around the Kanban board concept, where work items called cards move across columns called lists on a shared board. It was created in 2011 by Joel Spolsky and Michael Pryor at Fog Creek Software, spun out as an independent company in 2014, and acquired by Atlassian in January 2017 for roughly 425 million dollars.

Since the acquisition, Trello has remained Atlassian's entry-level, broadly accessible project management product, sitting alongside more complex tools like Jira, which is aimed at software development teams, and Confluence, Atlassian's documentation platform. Trello's core appeal is its simplicity: teams and individuals can start organizing work in minutes without training or setup.

Trello is used across a huge range of contexts, from personal to-do lists and household planning to marketing campaign tracking, editorial calendars, and lightweight software task boards, making it one of the most widely recognized project management tools on the market.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Trello offers drag-and-drop Kanban boards with customizable lists and cards that support checklists, due dates, labels, attachments, and comments. Butler, Trello's built-in automation engine, lets users create rule-based triggers, custom buttons, and scheduled commands without writing code and without paying extra, which is unusual among project management tools that often gate automation behind higher tiers.

Power-Ups extend Trello's functionality with integrations to tools like Slack, Google Drive, Microsoft Teams, and Atlassian's own Jira, letting boards connect to the rest of a team's software stack. Paid plans unlock additional board views beyond the classic Kanban layout, including Calendar, Timeline, Table, Dashboard, and Map views, giving teams more ways to visualize the same underlying data.

Enterprise customers gain organization-wide administrative controls, including centralized user management, advanced security policies, and permissions that span multiple workspaces, which matter for larger companies managing Trello usage across many teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Trello's Free plan includes up to 10 boards per workspace, 10 collaborators, and 250 Butler automation runs per month, making it usable for small teams without any payment. Standard costs 5 dollars per user per month billed annually and removes the board limit while adding advanced checklists and custom fields; Premium costs 10 dollars per user per month billed annually and adds Calendar, Timeline, Table, Dashboard, and Map views along with admin controls and unlimited automation.

Enterprise costs 17.50 dollars per user per month billed annually with a minimum of 50 users, and adds organization-wide permissions and advanced security features. Trello also offers discounts of up to 75 percent off paid plans for qualifying nonprofit organizations.

features.description: Connect boards to Slack, Google Drive, Jira, Microsoft Teams, and many other third-party tools.

features.title: Multiple board views
features.description: Switch between Calendar, Timeline, Table, Dashboard, and Map views on paid plans.

features.description: Start from pre-built templates for common workflows like sprint planning or content calendars.

features.description: Manage boards on the go with native iOS and Android applications.

Generous free plan usable indefinitely by small teams
Built-in Butler automation included at no extra cost on every plan
Wide ecosystem of Power-Up integrations with popular business tools
Backed by Atlassian's infrastructure, security, and long-term reliability
cons: Can become difficult to manage for large or highly complex projects
Native reporting and analytics are weaker than dedicated tools like Monday.com or Asana
Enterprise plan requires a 50-user minimum
Advanced views such as Timeline and Table are gated behind Premium
Limited built-in time tracking and resource management compared to full-featured PM suites

faqs.question: Is Trello free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Trello has a generous free plan supporting up to 10 boards per workspace, 10 collaborators, and 250 automation runs per month.

faqs.question: Who owns Trello?
faqs.answer: Trello is owned by Atlassian, which acquired it for approximately 425 million dollars in January 2017.

faqs.question: Who created Trello?
faqs.answer: Trello was created by Joel Spolsky and Michael Pryor at Fog Creek Software and launched in 2011.

faqs.question: What is Butler in Trello?
faqs.answer: Butler is Trello's built-in, no-code automation engine included free on every plan, including the Free tier.

faqs.question: What are Power-Ups?
faqs.answer: Power-Ups are Trello's app integrations and add-ons that connect boards to third-party tools like Slack and Google Drive.

faqs.question: How much does Trello Premium cost?
faqs.answer: Trello Premium costs 10 dollars per user per month when billed annually.

faqs.question: Does Trello have an Enterprise plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Enterprise starts at 17.50 dollars per user per month billed annually, with a minimum of 50 users.

faqs.question: Does Trello offer nonprofit discounts?
faqs.answer: Yes, qualifying nonprofits can receive up to 75 percent off Trello's paid plans.

enrichment.short_description: Trengo is a Netherlands-based omnichannel customer engagement platform that unifies email, WhatsApp, live chat, social media, and voice into one shared team inbox with AI-powered automation.
enrichment.seo_title: Trengo Review: Omnichannel Customer Engagement Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Trengo unifies WhatsApp, email, chat, and social channels into one inbox. See pricing plans from EUR125 a month, key features, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Trengo is an omnichannel customer engagement platform founded in 2017 and headquartered in Utrecht, Netherlands. The company has raised roughly 38 million dollars in funding, including a 36 million dollar Series A round backed by investors such as Insight Partners and Peak, and has grown to more than 50 employees serving customers primarily across Europe. Trengo's product combines a shared team inbox with WhatsApp Business API integration, live chat, email, social media channels such as Instagram and Facebook, and VoIP calling, all managed from a single unified interface alongside AI-powered chatbots and automation rules for routing and triaging conversations. This WhatsApp-first, channel-unifying approach has made Trengo especially popular with European e-commerce brands and small-to-midsize customer service and sales teams that rely heavily on messaging apps rather than traditional email or phone support. Published pricing includes three self-serve tiers priced in euros: Essentials at 125 euros a month for 5 users and 100 conversations, Boost at 185 euros a month for 5 users and 300 conversations, and Pro at 310 euros a month for 5 users and 1,000 conversations, plus a custom-priced Enterprise tier for larger deployments. Trengo is commonly compared to Front, Zendesk, Intercom, and HubSpot Service Hub; its main differentiator against these larger, more US-centric competitors is its deep, native WhatsApp Business integration and strong footprint among European and e-commerce customer service teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Trengo
paragraphs: Trengo is a customer engagement platform founded in 2017 and based in Utrecht, Netherlands, built to unify the many channels customers use to reach a business, including email, WhatsApp, live chat, social media, and voice, into a single shared inbox. Rather than forcing support and sales teams to juggle separate apps for each channel, Trengo consolidates conversations so a team can respond from one place regardless of where a message originated.

The platform has become particularly well known for its deep WhatsApp Business API integration, which is a major differentiator in European and e-commerce markets where WhatsApp is a dominant customer communication channel, something many US-focused helpdesk tools support less natively.

Trengo has raised significant venture funding, including a 36 million dollar Series A round, and serves small-to-midsize businesses through larger enterprise customers with tiered plans that scale by team size and monthly conversation volume.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of Trengo is a unified team inbox that pulls in messages from email, WhatsApp, live chat, Instagram, Facebook, and voice calls, letting agents see and respond to every conversation without switching tools. The WhatsApp Business API integration supports two-way messaging, broadcast campaigns, and automated flows, which is central to Trengo's appeal for e-commerce and consumer-facing brands.

AI-powered chatbots and rule-based automation handle first-line triage, routing, and frequently asked questions before a conversation reaches a human agent, while internal notes, assignment rules, and service-level agreement tracking help teams collaborate on shared conversations without confusion.

Trengo also includes a reporting and analytics dashboard for tracking response times and conversation volume, along with integrations to e-commerce and CRM platforms such as Shopify and HubSpot, letting customer context flow directly into support conversations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Trengo's published plans are priced in euros and scale by both team size and monthly conversation volume. Essentials starts at 125 euros a month for 5 users and 100 conversations, Boost is 185 euros a month for 5 users and 300 conversations, and Pro is 310 euros a month for 5 users and 1,000 conversations; a custom-priced Enterprise plan is available for larger teams with higher volume or advanced security requirements.

Because all plans have a 5-user minimum and price out additional conversation volume, smaller teams that need more than the included conversation allowance, or teams larger than 5 users, should expect the effective monthly cost to rise beyond the listed starting price.

AI-powered automation reduces manual conversation triage

Scales from small teams up to a custom Enterprise tier

No permanent free plan
Pricing is listed primarily in euros, adding currency conversion considerations outside Europe
Fewer native integrations than larger competitors such as Zendesk or Intercom
Advanced automation setup has a learning curve for new teams

enrichment.short_description: Trigger.dev is an open-source TypeScript platform for building and running long-running background jobs, AI agent workflows, and durable tasks with built-in retries, queues, and observability.
enrichment.seo_title: Trigger.dev Review: Open-Source Background Jobs Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Trigger.dev is an open-source TypeScript platform for background jobs and AI workflows. See pricing from 10 dollars a month and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Trigger.dev was founded in 2022 by Matt Aitken, Eric Allam, Dan Patel, and James Ritchie, and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom. The company went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch and raised a seed round in the range of 3 to 3.5 million dollars. Trigger.dev is an open-source platform, with its core released under an open-source license and self-hostable, built specifically for developers writing TypeScript who need to run long-running background jobs, durable tasks, and AI agent workflows reliably, complete with automatic retries, task queues, elastic scaling, and full observability and tracing of every run. It is distinct from general-purpose distributed workflow engines such as Temporal in that it is TypeScript-first, ships as a fully-managed cloud platform out of the box, and is explicitly positioned around AI agent and background job use cases rather than being a broad, language-agnostic orchestration engine. The free plan includes 5 dollars of monthly usage credits along with 20 concurrent runs, 10 schedules, and 1 day of log retention; the Hobby plan is 10 dollars a month with more credits and higher limits; the Pro plan is 50 dollars a month with 200 or more concurrent runs, 1,000 or more schedules, 30-day log retention, and dedicated Slack support; and an Enterprise tier offers custom log retention, single sign-on, and SOC 2 and penetration test reports. Beyond the base plan fee, usage is billed per second of task compute time plus a small per-run invocation fee. Trigger.dev is commonly compared to Inngest, Temporal, and no-code automation tools like Zapier or Make, but it specifically targets engineering teams building code-first background jobs and AI agents rather than non-technical business users.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Trigger.dev
paragraphs: Trigger.dev is an open-source platform for building and running background jobs, durable long-running tasks, and AI agent workflows in TypeScript. It was founded in 2022 by Matt Aitken, Eric Allam, Dan Patel, and James Ritchie in London and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch before raising a seed round backed by early-stage investors.

The platform is aimed squarely at software engineering teams that need to run code reliably outside the request-response cycle of a typical web server, such as long-running AI agent tasks, scheduled jobs, data pipeline steps, and webhook processing that can take minutes or hours rather than milliseconds.

Its core is open source and can be self-hosted, while most users run their jobs on Trigger.dev's fully-managed cloud, which handles infrastructure, scaling, retries, and observability so engineering teams do not need to build and operate their own job queue and worker infrastructure from scratch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Trigger.dev provides a TypeScript-native SDK for defining tasks, jobs, and workflows directly in application code, with automatic retries and durable execution so a task can recover from a failure or a deploy without losing progress. Task queues allow developers to control concurrency precisely, and elastic, fully-managed infrastructure scales workers up and down automatically based on load.

Every run is fully observable, with built-in tracing and detailed logs that make it possible to debug long-running or multi-step tasks the same way a developer would debug a normal function call. The platform also supports scheduled and recurring tasks using familiar cron-style syntax, alongside first-class support for building AI agent workflows.

Because the core SDK and server are open source, teams that need to keep everything in their own infrastructure can self-host Trigger.dev rather than relying on the managed cloud, while still getting the same developer experience and tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Trigger.dev's cloud pricing combines a monthly plan fee with usage-based compute and invocation costs. The Free plan includes 5 dollars of monthly credits, 20 concurrent runs, 10 schedules, and 1 day of log retention; Hobby is 10 dollars a month with more credits and higher limits; and Pro is 50 dollars a month with 200 or more concurrent runs, 1,000 or more schedules, 30-day log retention, and dedicated Slack support.

Beyond the included monthly credits, tasks are billed per second of execution time based on compute size, plus a small per-run invocation fee of about 0.000025 dollars per run. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with extended log retention, single sign-on, and compliance reports such as SOC 2 for organizations with stricter requirements.

features.title: TypeScript-native SDK

features.description: Failed tasks automatically retry and can resume durably without losing progress.

features.title: Task queues with concurrency control

features.description: Workers scale automatically based on demand without manual infrastructure management.

features.title: Built-in observability and tracing
features.description: Every run is logged and traceable for debugging long-running or multi-step tasks.

features.title: Scheduled and recurring tasks
features.description: Run jobs on cron-style schedules alongside event-triggered tasks.

features.title: AI agent workflow support
features.description: Purpose-built primitives for running long-lived AI agent tasks reliably.

features.title: Self-hostable open-source core
features.description: Run Trigger.dev on your own infrastructure instead of the managed cloud if required.

pros: Open source with a genuinely self-hostable option
TypeScript-first developer experience tailored to modern web and AI stacks
Generous free plan with real usage credits rather than a hard feature cap
Purpose-built for long-running and AI agent workflows, not just simple queues
Backed by Y Combinator with active, ongoing development
cons: Small, young company relative to established workflow platforms
Usage-based compute pricing can be difficult to forecast at scale
Requires TypeScript and coding knowledge, unlike no-code automation tools
Fewer pre-built app integrations than no-code platforms like Zapier or Make
Smaller community and ecosystem than more established job frameworks

faqs.answer: It is an open-source TypeScript platform for building background jobs, durable tasks, and AI agent workflows.

faqs.answer: Yes, there is a free plan that includes monthly usage credits and core features, with paid plans starting at 10 dollars a month for higher limits.

faqs.question: Who founded Trigger.dev?

faqs.question: Is Trigger.dev open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its core platform is open source and can be self-hosted in addition to using the managed cloud.

faqs.answer: Yes, it went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 (W23) batch.

faqs.question: How is Trigger.dev priced?
faqs.answer: Pricing combines a monthly plan fee with usage-based compute charges billed per second of execution, plus a small per-run invocation fee.

faqs.question: How does Trigger.dev differ from Temporal?
faqs.answer: Trigger.dev is TypeScript-first with a fully-managed cloud and simpler developer experience, aimed at web and AI engineering teams, rather than being a general-purpose, language-agnostic workflow engine.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Trigger.dev?
faqs.answer: Yes, its open-source core can be self-hosted for teams that need to keep everything on their own infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: Trilium Notes is a free, open-source, self-hosted hierarchical note-taking application for building large personal knowledge bases, now maintained by the community as TriliumNext.
enrichment.seo_title: Trilium Notes Review: Open-Source Hierarchical Note App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Trilium Notes is a free, self-hosted, open-source note-taking app with unlimited hierarchy, note cloning, and a WYSIWYG editor. See features and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Trilium Notes was originally created by a developer known as zadam and first publicly released on December 25, 2017, as a free, open-source, self-hosted note-taking application under the AGPL-3.0 license, built for creating large personal knowledge bases. It is often positioned as a privacy-respecting, self-hosted alternative to Evernote, OneNote, and Roam Research. Its signature design choice is organizing notes as an unlimited-depth hierarchical tree rather than flat notebooks or folders, and it supports note cloning, which lets a single note appear in multiple places in that hierarchy without duplicating its content. All data is stored locally in a SQLite database, and users can optionally run a self-hosted sync server to keep notes consistent across devices, with no cloud dependency, subscription fee, or telemetry involved. The editor is a WYSIWYG interface supporting Markdown-style shortcuts, tables, images, math expressions, and syntax-highlighted code blocks, and the application also includes a built-in calendar view, a Kanban board view, and a browser web-clipper extension for saving pages and screenshots directly into the note tree. In January 2024, the original developer moved the project into maintenance mode, prompting a group of community contributors led by Elian Doran to fork the project as TriliumNext to keep active development going. The fork grew to roughly 2,700 stars independently, and the original repository, which had accumulated around 22,000 stars, was later transferred to the TriliumNext organization, which now maintains the project going forward simply as Trilium Notes. As fully free, self-hosted, open-source software, Trilium Notes has no pricing tiers or paid version.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Trilium Notes
paragraphs: Trilium Notes is a free, open-source, self-hosted note-taking application designed for building large, deeply organized personal knowledge bases. It was first released in December 2017 by a developer known as zadam and has since become a well-known alternative to commercial note apps like Evernote, OneNote, and Notion for users who want full control over their data.

The application's defining feature is its hierarchical structure: instead of storing notes in flat notebooks or folders, Trilium organizes them as a tree of unlimited depth, and any note can be cloned to appear in multiple branches of that tree simultaneously without duplicating its underlying content. This makes it possible to build genuinely large, cross-referenced knowledge bases that stay navigable over time.

In January 2024, the original creator moved the project into maintenance mode, and a group of community contributors organized as TriliumNext to fork the project and keep it actively developed. That community fork eventually reunited with the original repository when zadam transferred ownership to the TriliumNext organization, which now continues developing and releasing the project as Trilium Notes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Trilium's core editor is a WYSIWYG interface that supports Markdown-style shortcuts and slash commands for quick formatting, alongside tables, images, math expressions, and code blocks with syntax highlighting, making it capable of handling both everyday notes and technical documentation. Notes can be tagged with labels and connected through relations, giving users a way to add structured metadata beyond the tree hierarchy alone.

Beyond plain note-taking, Trilium includes a built-in calendar for organizing events with day, week, month, and year views, and a Kanban board mode for tracking tasks or project status directly inside the note tree. A browser-based web-clipper extension lets users capture web pages or screenshots and file them straight into their knowledge base.

All data is stored in a local SQLite database rather than a proprietary cloud format, and users who want to access their notes across multiple devices can run a self-hosted sync server, keeping full control of their data without relying on any third-party service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Trilium Notes is entirely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with no subscription fees, license keys, or paid tiers of any kind. Since it is self-hosted or run locally, the only associated cost is optional hosting infrastructure if a user chooses to run a personal sync server; development is sustained by the open-source community rather than a commercial revenue model.

features.description: Place a single note in multiple locations in the hierarchy without duplicating its content.

features.title: Code blocks and math expressions
features.description: Write technical notes with syntax-highlighted code blocks and inline math support.

features.title: Built-in calendar view
features.description: Organize events with day, week, month, and year calendar views inside the app.

features.title: Kanban board view
features.description: Track tasks or project status directly within the note hierarchy using a Kanban layout.

features.description: Add structured metadata and connections between notes beyond the tree structure alone.

features.description: Keep all data locally, with an optional self-hosted server for syncing across devices.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscription cost
Strong privacy through local-first storage with no telemetry or vendor lock-in
Highly flexible unlimited-depth hierarchy with powerful note cloning
Actively developed again after the TriliumNext community revival
Well suited to large, deeply cross-referenced personal knowledge bases
cons: Steeper learning curve than simple flat note apps due to the hierarchy and relations model
No official hosted cloud service, requiring self-hosting or local setup for cross-device sync
Interface feels more technical and less polished than consumer apps like Notion
Mobile experience has historically lagged behind the desktop and server experience
The project underwent a maintenance-mode scare in 2024 before the community fork took over

faqs.question: Is Trilium Notes free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Trilium Notes is completely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Who maintains Trilium Notes today?
faqs.answer: The TriliumNext community organization maintains it, after original creator zadam moved the project to maintenance mode in 2024 and later transferred repository ownership.

faqs.question: Does Trilium Notes require the cloud?
faqs.answer: No, all data is stored locally in a SQLite database, with an optional self-hosted sync server for accessing notes on multiple devices.

faqs.question: How is Trilium different from Notion or Evernote?
faqs.answer: Trilium is self-hosted and open source, and it organizes notes in an unlimited-depth tree with note cloning rather than flat pages or notebooks.

faqs.question: Can I use Trilium Notes on mobile?
faqs.answer: Yes, though the desktop and server experience has traditionally been more feature-complete than mobile.

faqs.question: Does Trilium Notes support Markdown?
faqs.answer: Yes, the editor supports Markdown-style shortcuts as well as Markdown import and export.

faqs.question: Is there a web clipper for Trilium Notes?
faqs.answer: Yes, a browser extension lets users save web pages and screenshots directly into their note hierarchy.

faqs.question: What license does Trilium Notes use?
faqs.answer: It is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0).
